identity.short_name: Engineered remainder \to quadratic probe resultant product

problem_spec.summary: Given a monic cubic P with integer coefficients and roots r_i, define an engineered expression E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x) where Q is a fixed quadratic probe (often x^2+1). Then E(r_i)=Q(r_i) for all roots. The desired product over roots equals Res(P,Q), which can be computed by evaluating P at the roots of Q (a conjugate pair), then reducing modulo 1000.
givens: P(x) is a monic cubic in ℤ[x] with complex roots r_1,r_2,r_3 counted with multiplicity.
Q(x) is a fixed quadratic probe in ℤ[x] (in this instance Q(x)=x^2+1).
E(x) is defined by E(x)=x \cdot P(x)+Q(x) (so deg(E)=4 and E\equivQ (mod P)).

parameter(s).name: cubic_coefficients
parameter(s).domain: monic cubic x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c with a,b,c \in ℤ
parameter(s).purpose: defines the root constraint ring ℤ[x]/(P) and the multiset of roots r_i

parameter(s).name: quadratic_probe
parameter(s).domain: integer-coefficient quadratic Q(x) with deg(Q)=2 (often chosen to have conjugate roots)
parameter(s).purpose: the reduced expression whose values are multiplied over the roots of P

parameter(s).name: engineered_expression_form
parameter(s).purpose: forces the remainder of E modulo P to be exactly Q, making reduction immediate and structural

invariant(s).name: engineered_remainder_identity
invariant(s).statement: Since E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), we have E(x) \equiv Q(x) (mod P(x)), hence for every root r of P, E(r)=Q(r).

invariant(s).name: resultant_equals_product_over_roots
invariant(s).statement: For monic P, Res(P,Q)=_{P(r)=0} Q(r) (counted with multiplicity), so the target product equals a resultant.

invariant(s).name: integer_valued_symmetry
invariant(s).statement: Res(P,Q) is an integer polynomial in the coefficients of P and Q; therefore the product is an integer.

essential_assumption(s): Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: P has exactly three complex roots counted with multiplicity.
Work in characteristic 0 so resultants and division algorithm behave as usual.
The product over roots uses multiplicity (standard convention), matching the resultant identity.
uniqueness_certificate(s): Because E(x)=xP(x)+Q(x), the product  E(r_i) equals  Q(r_i)=Res(P,Q). The resultant is uniquely determined by the coefficients of P and Q, hence the integer product is unique. Reducing modulo m gives a unique residue in {0,...,m-1}.

technique(s).name: engineered_remainder_reduction
technique(s).purpose: reduce E(r) to Q(r) instantly for all roots r of P

technique(s).name: resultant_swap_to_probe_roots
inputs: quadratic probe Q with roots α,β and leading coefficient a_Q

technique(s).name: conjugate_norm_elimination
technique(s).purpose: multiply conjugates to remove radicals/imaginary parts and obtain an integer

technique(s).name: modular_postprocessing
technique(s).purpose: report the residue class modulo m

theorem(s).name: Polynomial Division Algorithm
theorem(s).role: guarantees remainder congruence and evaluation compatibility modulo P at roots

theorem(s).name: Resultant identity
theorem(s).role: Res(P,Q)=a_P^{deg Q} Q(r_i)=a_Q^{deg P} P(roots of Q)

theorem(s).name: Conjugate product (norm)
theorem(s).role: (u+vi)(u-vi)=u^2+v^2 (and similarly for quadratic radicals), ensuring integrality of the product

critical_trick(s): Recognize/construct E(x) so that E(r)=Q(r) for P(r)=0 (engineered remainder).
Convert the product over cubic roots into evaluation of P at the two quadratic-probe roots via a resultant.
Use conjugate multiplication to eliminate i (or \sqrt\Delta) and obtain an integer, then reduce mod 1000.

library(s).name: sympy
library(s).reason: exact polynomial remainder/resultant computations for verification and regression testing

task(s).name: compute_resultant_directly
task(s).reason: verify T_full = resultant(P,E) = resultant(P,Q) exactly in ℤ

task(s).name: verify_engineered_remainder
task(s).reason: confirm E - xP - Q is identically zero and hence E\equivQ (mod P)

task(s).name: reduce_mod_m
task(s).reason: compute T_full mod 1000 reliably with exact arithmetic
